variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 at 25% compounding interest, due immediately in full, with wage garnishment and asset seizure enforced upon any missed payment.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 at 18% interest with a strict 12-month repayment deadline and severe financial penalties for any late payment.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 at 10% interest with a firm 3-year repayment schedule and late fees applied to missed payments.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 at 3% interest with a flexible 10-year repayment plan and no penalties for occasional late payments.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 with no interest, a generous 20-year repayment window, and the option to adjust payments based on your income.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe someone $100,000 with no interest, no fixed deadline, and the understanding that any portion you cannot repay will eventually be forgiven.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier modifies the harshness of the debt terms while preserving the core scenario of owing $100,000. Tier 1 features punitive interest, immediate full repayment, and aggressive enforcement. Each successive tier relaxes terms via lower interest rates, longer timelines, fewer penalties, and greater flexibility, culminating in Tier 7's forgivable, interest-free, open-ended arrangement.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a debt with no interest, no fixed deadline, and eventual forgiveness is the mildest possible version of owing $100,000—minimizing financial burden and stress for the debtor.
